name: pipal
title: pipal

commands.name: pipal

arguments.raw: --top, -t X: show the top X results (default 10)
--gkey <Google Maps API key>: to allow zip code lookups (optional)

arguments.description: Show the available checkers and which are enabled
arguments.raw: --list-checkers: Show the available checkers and which are enabled

local_help.--help:
pipal 3.4.0 Robin Wood (robin@digi.ninja) (http://digi.ninja)

Usage: pipal [OPTION] ... FILENAME
	--help, -h, -?: show help
	--top, -t X: show the top X results (default 10)
	--output, -o <filename>: output to file
	--gkey <Google Maps API key>: to allow zip code lookups (optional)
	--list-checkers: Show the available checkers and which are enabled
	--verbose, -v: Verbose


	FILENAME: The file to count

binary_metadata.file_type: a sh script, ASCII text executable

apt_metadata.description:
Statistical analysis on password dumps
All this tool does is to give you the stats and the
information to help you analyse the passwords. The real
work is done by you in interpreting the results.

use_cases: When you need Kali Linux, use pipal because ### pipal.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `pipal [OPTION] ... FILENAME` as documented upstream for pipal.
When chaining Kali Linux tools, pair pipal with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `pipal --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.

documentation.how_to_use: Install/setup: sudo apt install pipal. Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `pipal [OPTION] ... FILENAME`.
documentation.when_to_use: Use during the 'any' phase against surfaces [web] in the Kali Linux category. Fits when the engagement needs: ### pipal.
usage_sections: Install with: sudo apt install pipal
Install with: sudo apt install pipal
